1: The fourth Servant Song (Isaiah 52:13-53:12) has been interpreted in Jewish tradition as: (1) collective Israel suffering in exile and ultimately vindicated; (2) the righteous remnant of Israel; (3) an individual historical figure (Jeremiah, Moses, Hezekiah, or a suffering prophet). Early Jewish sources (1QIs, Targum Jonathan, b. Sanhedrin 98b where some rabbis applied it to the Messiah) show a live debate. The NT unanimously applies the Servant to Jesus (Matthew 8:17; Luke 22:37; John 12:38; Acts 8:32; Romans 10:16; 1 Peter 2:22-24; Revelation 5:6). The linguistic data: the Servant suffers vicariously ('for our sins', 'for our transgressions', 'the LORD laid on him the iniquity of us all'), is innocent ('no violence or deceit'), is exalted after suffering ('he shall see his offspring, he shall prolong his days'), and his suffering is efficacious ('by his knowledge shall the righteous one, my servant, make many to be accounted righteous').
2: The Servant grew up before YHWH like a tender shoot and like a root from dry soil. The plant-from-dry-ground image evokes the Davidic stump of Isaiah 11:1 — a surviving root in apparently dead earth. Dry soil connotes the hostile political and spiritual climate: the Babylonian exile was not fertile ground for messianic emergence. The total absence of physical attractiveness is a deliberate anti-royal convention — Samuel's kings (Saul, the young David) were conspicuously handsome; the Servant's emergence bypasses the conventional honor markers of kingship.
3: Despised and rejected by people — a man who knew suffering, familiar with grief. The honor-shame framework of the ANE made public rejection the most devastating social verdict possible. Hiding the face was a ritual act of avoidance: contact with one shamed or ritually impure transferred dishonor. By hiding their faces, the observers treat the Servant as a source of shame-contagion to be avoided rather than a figure to be honored. The community's failure to value him mirrors the exile community's failure to recognize YHWH's work through apparently defeated agents.
4: Yet he was the one who took up our sicknesses and carried our pain. The assumption being corrected — struck down and afflicted by God — was the standard ANE and Israelite medical theology: bodily suffering signaled divine punishment for sin (cf. Job's friends; John 9:2). The Servant's suffering appears to the observers to confirm this logic. The passage then subverts it: the illness and pain are real, but they are ours, not his. The substitutionary transfer is the theological shock.
5: He was pierced because of our transgressions — crushed because of our iniquities. The punishment that brings us peace (shalom) fell on him, and by his wounds we are healed. The verb pierced (meḥolal) refers to a lethal wound. Crushing (daka) implies total destruction. Both are capital-punishment terms. The causal logic is explicit: the transgressions and iniquities are ours; the piercing and crushing are his. The word for wounds (chavurato) denotes the bruised, lacerated wound of flogging — the same punishment as the beating described in Isaiah 50:6. The healing that results inverts the wound-as-divine-curse logic entirely.
6: We all went astray like sheep — each turned to his own way — and YHWH laid on him the iniquity of us all. The sheep metaphor captures both the innocence and the helplessness of the straying community: sheep do not rebel deliberately but drift away individually. The verb laid on (hifgi'a bo) is the same root used for an intercessor who brings another's case to YHWH — here YHWH himself is both the one who intercedes and the one who bears. The corporate guilt of the entire community is concentrated and transferred to the single figure of the Servant.
7: Oppressed and afflicted, yet he never opened his mouth — like a lamb led to slaughter, like a sheep silent before its shearers. The Passover lamb and wool-industry sheep were both familiar agricultural images. In the ANE, silence before accusers and executioners was not the dignified silence of an innocent man but the political impotence of someone without legal recourse. The Servant's silence is therefore both voluntary and deliberate — not a lack of defense options but a chosen non-response that enables the substitutionary action.
8: By a perversion of justice (oser u-mishpat) he was taken away — cut off from the land of the living, struck for the transgression of my people. The legal irregularity of the proceedings is noted: justice was bent, not applied properly. Being cut off from the land of the living is the Psalmic phrase for execution or untimely death. No official mourning was observed — who gave any thought to his fate implies no lamentation ritual, no proper burial honor, no communal response to his death. The struck-for-others logic is here made explicit by YHWH himself as speaker.
9: He was assigned a grave among the wicked yet his burial was with the rich — though he had done no violence and there was no deceit in his mouth. Executed criminals in the ANE were buried dishonorably, often in mass graves outside the city. The reversal — a rich man's burial — is historically remarkable and treated as significant by the text. The dual innocence declaration (no violence, no deceit) uses the two categories most associated with criminal activity: physical aggression and deceptive speech. The Servant's combination of criminal execution and innocent character produces the paradox the text insists upon.
10: Yet it was YHWH's will to crush him and make him suffer. When his life is given as a guilt offering (asham), he will see his offspring and prolong his days. The asham is the specific sacrificial category of Leviticus 5-6 for unintentional sins requiring both atonement and restitution — distinct from the burnt offering and the sin offering. The guilt offering was brought by the offender to cover the specific damage caused by the violation. Applying this category to the Servant positions him as the sacrifice that covers humanity's specific debt to YHWH. Seeing offspring and prolonging days after death is the resurrection logic embedded in the text.
11: Out of the anguish of his life he will see and be satisfied — by his knowledge my righteous servant will make many righteous and will bear their iniquities. The Servant's own knowledge (da'ato) is the instrument of justification — not merely mechanical sacrifice but the Servant's understanding and deliberate action that makes many righteous. Bearing iniquities is the priestly substitution vocabulary of Leviticus: the priest bore the sin of the community in the atonement ritual. The Servant combines the priestly and sacrificial roles: both the one who offers and the offering itself.
12: Therefore I will give him a portion among the great and he will share the plunder with the strong — because he poured out his life unto death and was counted with the transgressors, for he bore the sin of many and makes intercession for the transgressors. The military-plunder distribution imagery frames the Servant's reward: he receives the share due to a victorious warrior. His being counted with transgressors is the criminal's execution; the intercession for transgressors is an ongoing priestly activity in the present tense. Luke 22:37 explicitly quotes Isaiah 53:12 as fulfilled at Jesus's crucifixion between two criminals.
